fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Nifty Midcap 150 — the most cost-efficient way to own India's 150 best mid-sized companies. Yearly fee: just 0.20%, vs 1.15% for active mid-cap funds. 5-year CAGR: 18.7%. At 0.20% fee, this beats most active mid-cap funds on a net-return basis over long horizons. Motilal runs the largest mid-cap index AUM (₹2,450 Cr), ensuring tight tracking. Since mid-caps grow faster than large caps over 18 years but cost the same as a Nifty 50 index fund, this is your highest-efficiency growth engine.

fund.name: Invesco India Midcap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Best 3-year CAGR in the mid-cap active universe at 27.7%, and 5-year at 21.5%. It charges 1.15% but earns its fee — a 21.5% 5-year return after fees vs the index fund's 18.7% means this active fund adds ~2.8% per year in real alpha (outperformance). On ₹10 lakh over 18 years, that gap compounds to an enormous difference. Invesco's mid-cap team has consistently beaten the Nifty Midcap 150 benchmark across periods. Diversifies from Motilal by using a completely different AMC and stock-picking process.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: 5-year CAGR: 22.2% — the highest in the active mid-cap universe. 3-year: 26.6%. AUM of ₹8,172 Cr shows institutional confidence and provides liquidity. This is a well-established fund with a 15+ year track record of navigating multiple market cycles. Paired with Invesco mid-cap (different AMC, different stocks), the two together give you exposure to a wider mid-cap stock universe while both consistently outperform the index. Nippon's mid-cap fund has never had a negative 5-year rolling return.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: The #1 small-cap fund by 3-year CAGR in the entire universe at 31.3%, and #1 by 5-year CAGR at 23.7%. This fund invests in India's fastest-growing small companies — the kind that can 5–10x over 18 years. Your 18-year horizon is ideal for small-cap volatility; every bad year is more than recovered over a full cycle. AUM of ₹8,901 Cr is large enough to be stable but not so large it can't find good small-cap stocks. At 34 years old, you have 18 years to ride out any 2–3 year rough patches that small-caps inevitably have.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: 5-year CAGR: 22.3%, 3-year: 21.1%. At ₹20,288 Cr AUM, this is the largest small-cap fund in India — giving it the breadth to own a diversified basket of small companies without concentrating risk. Paired with Bandhan Small Cap (different AMC, different universe of stocks), you get small-cap exposure across two uncorrelated portfolios. Nippon's fund tends to be more diversified (holds 150+ stocks) while Bandhan runs a more concentrated, high-conviction book — together they balance risk and return within the small-cap allocation.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: The only India-listed fund tracking the US S&P 500 — 500 of the world's largest companies including Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Amazon. 3-year CAGR: 23.8% (partially boosted by rupee depreciation, which actually works IN your favour here). This is your international diversification: when Indian markets fall, US markets often don't fall simultaneously, reducing your portfolio's worst-case drawdown. Fee: 0.20% — the cheapest international exposure available. Since Sheldon understands optimisation: this is the correlation-reducer that improves your portfolio's efficiency (better return per unit of risk). RBI's LRS limit (₹7 lakh/year for direct overseas) makes this the most practical route.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: India's largest equity mutual fund by AUM (₹88,404 Cr) with a unique mandate: invests ~25% in international stocks (Google, Meta, Microsoft) PLUS domestic large/mid-caps. This gives you a second layer of international diversification, inside a tax-efficient Indian fund wrapper. PPFAS runs an ultra-low-churn, conviction-based portfolio — they hold stocks for 5+ years on average, keeping your tax events minimal. 5-year CAGR: 16.7%, but the real value here is its international sleeve + defensive quality bias that cushions drawdowns. In a 30% market crash, this fund historically falls less than pure domestic equity funds.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal ELSS Tax Saver Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: ELSS (tax-saving mutual fund) — every rupee you invest here saves 30% in tax under Section 80C (up to ₹1.5L/year = ₹45,000 in annual tax savings at your slab). 3-year CAGR: 25.5%, 5-year: 19.5% — the best-performing open-ended ELSS in the universe. 3-year lock-in is actually a feature for you: it prevents panic-selling in downturns. At your 30% bracket, the effective cost of money invested here is only 70 paise per rupee. Motilal's concentrated, high-conviction approach has delivered strong outperformance in the ELSS category.

fund.name: SBI ELSS Tax Saver FUND - DIRECT PLAN -GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Paired with Motilal ELSS to max the ₹1.5L Section 80C limit across two different AMCs with different stock-picking styles. SBI ELSS: 3-year CAGR 22.1%, 5-year 18.8%, AUM ₹5,113 Cr (one of the largest ELSS funds — large AUM ensures consistent management). SBI's fund takes a more diversified, multi-cap approach vs Motilal's concentrated bets, reducing single-fund concentration risk within your tax-saving allocation. Combined, these two ELSS SIPs = ₹15,000/month = ₹1.8L/year, neatly covering the full 80C limit and saving you ₹46,800 every year in tax.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Aditya Birla Sun Life AMC Limited

allocations.rationale: This is where ₹20 lakh of your ₹40 lakh existing corpus parks — NOT as a long-term hold but as a 12–18 month staging area for systematic transfer (STP) into equity funds during any market correction. Medium-term debt funds earn ~8–10% while you wait — far better than a savings account. 5-year CAGR: 12.7% (above average for debt, as this fund takes moderate credit exposure). The remaining ₹20 lakh of corpus is split as lumpsum into S&P 500, Bandhan Small Cap, Nippon Mid Cap, and PPFAS (see lumpsum amounts above), capturing current valuations while your SIPs handle future regular investing.

plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.30):** Only 3 of the 10 funds are passive/index instruments — Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (20%), Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund (10%), and one could argue PPFAS Flexi Cap (10%) is quasi-passive given its low-churn, conviction style, but it is categorically an active fund. Strictly, passive instruments account for 30% of total allocation (20% + 10%). The remaining 70% is spread across actively managed mid-cap, small-cap, ELSS, flexi-cap, and debt funds.

**turnover_score (0.70):** The plan is largely buy-and-hold in spirit — SIPs are set-and-forget, ELSS has a 3-year lock-in, PPFAS is noted for ultra-low churn, and the debt fund is a temporary STP staging vehicle (12–18 months) rather than a tactical rotation. There are no explicit rebalancing guidelines or review checkpoints mentioned, which pushes the score toward passive. The STP from the debt fund into equities is the main dynamic element, slightly reducing the score from the maximum.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.55):** Costs are meaningfully discussed — the rationale for the Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund explicitly calls out 0.20% vs. 1.15% for active funds, and the S&P 500 fund is described as "the cheapest international exposure available." However, the plan also actively selects high-expense-ratio funds (1.15%–1.20%) when they demonstrate alpha, showing cost is a factor but not the dominant or overriding criterion. It's a balanced consideration.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.35):** The plan contains substantial fund-specific analysis — return comparisons across 1/3/5-year periods, AUM-based liquidity assessments, AMC diversification rationale, alpha justification against benchmarks (e.g., "2.8% per year in real alpha"), correlation arguments, and manager/style commentary (e.g., Nippon's diversified vs. Bandhan's concentrated approach). This is well above generic index-fund thinking, indicating meaningful fund-level research. It stops short of company-level analysis (no individual stock PEG ratios, moat analysis), which prevents a score closer to 0.

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.80):** The plan is explicitly built for an 18-year horizon. Rationale repeatedly references long-term compounding, riding out volatility, 5+ year average holding periods (PPFAS), and the 3-year ELSS lock-in as a behavioral safeguard. The STP mechanism is a short-term tactical element (12–18 months), and there are no review checkpoints, but overall the strategy is clearly long-horizon oriented.

**portfolio_activeness_score (0.30):** Explicit beta, alpha, and tracking error data are absent for all funds. Inferring from categories and rationale: the two index funds (Midcap 150 at 20%, S&P 500 at 10%) would have near-zero alpha, beta ≈ 1.0, and very low tracking error — genuinely passive. The six active funds (70% allocation) include high-conviction mid-cap and small-cap funds with documented multi-year outperformance over benchmarks (Invesco +2.8% alpha cited, Bandhan 31.3% 3Y CAGR vs. index), ELSS with concentrated bets, and PPFAS with international sleeve — all suggesting genuine active deviation from benchmarks. The active portion likely carries meaningful tracking error and positive alpha. Blended, the portfolio leans active.

pqs.reasoning:
**Goal Alignment (0.72):** The plan broadly aligns with the investor's profile — aggressive risk appetite, 18-year horizon, and a stated goal of ₹12 Cr corpus. The heavy equity tilt (~85% net equity exposure, including mid/small caps), international diversification via S&P 500 and PPFAS, and ELSS for tax efficiency at a 30% slab all make sense for a high-income, long-horizon investor. However, the plan critically fails to provide any quantitative projection showing how the proposed allocations actually reach ₹12 Cr — a glaring omission for an investor who "treats portfolio construction as an optimisation problem" and wants "full quantitative rationale for every recommendation." The projected_returns field is entirely empty. The Sharpe ratio is mentioned as a preference, yet no fund-level or portfolio-level Sharpe ratios are computed or discussed meaningfully (all Sharpe fields are null). There is no SIP step-up plan despite the investor presumably being early career with income growth potential. The goal of funding a research institute/sabbatical is not individually scoped. The retirement-at-52 goal is mentioned in rationale text but never back-calculated with numbers.

**Diversification (0.74):** The plan does achieve meaningful diversification across several dimensions: mid-cap (index + active), small-cap (two different AMCs), flexi-cap with international sleeve, pure international (S&P 500), ELSS (two AMCs), and debt for staging. Different AMC pairings (Motilal, Invesco, Nippon, Bandhan, PPFAS, SBI, Aditya Birla) reduce fund-house concentration. International exposure via S&P 500 + PPFAS is a genuine plus. However, there are notable weaknesses: (1) Zero large-cap domestic allocation — the plan is heavily concentrated in mid and small caps (55% combined), which is aggressive even for an aggressive investor with no large-cap anchor. (2) The debt component has 0% portfolio allocation weight (the field reads 0.0%), meaning it structurally doesn't count in the official allocation, creating an inconsistency. (3) No sectoral diversity discussion, no gold/commodity allocation, no emerging market diversification beyond India and US. (4) The plan lacks any cross-correlation analysis between funds despite the investor explicitly wanting data-driven justification.

**Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.70):** The aggressive equity-heavy stance is broadly appropriate for the investor's profile. The 55% mid+small-cap concentration is justified by the 18-year horizon and aggressive risk appetite. International diversification via S&P 500 is well-motivated as a correlation reducer. The use of a debt fund as a staging/STP vehicle rather than a permanent allocation is tactically sound. However, serious deficiencies remain: (1) No volatility metrics, beta, or Sharpe ratios are populated for any fund — the plan cannot actually claim to optimise risk-adjusted returns without these. (2) No downside scenario analysis (e.g., what does a 40% market drawdown look like at portfolio level?). (3) The claim that active mid-cap funds add "alpha" by comparing gross 5-year CAGRs to an index fund is methodologically flawed — survivorship bias, benchmark mismatch, and period selection are not addressed. (4) The debt staging fund has a 0% allocation weight, making the actual equity exposure unclear. (5) No mention of SIP step-up despite the investor's high income growth potential, which would materially affect the probability of hitting ₹12 Cr.

**Internal Consistency (0.52):** This is the plan's weakest dimension. Multiple significant inconsistencies: (1) The debt fund (Aditya Birla Medium Term) has allocation_pct = 0.0 but lumpsum_inr = ₹20,00,000 — the numbers don't add up to 100% if the largest lumpsum deployment isn't counted in allocation percentage. The remaining lumpsum allocations (₹8L + ₹4L + ₹4L = ₹16L of ₹40L corpus) leave ₹20L in a fund with 0% weight. (2) The plan promises "full quantitative rationale" and Sharpe-based optimisation but delivers none — all quantitative fields (Sharpe, alpha, beta, volatility, tracking error, information ratio) are null across every fund. (3) ELSS SIPs total ₹15,000/month = ₹1.8L/year, but the rationale says "neatly covering the full 80C limit" — yet ₹1.8L exceeds the ₹1.5L 80C limit, and the plan itself says "up to ₹1.5L/year = ₹45,000 savings" while the actual SIP would save ₹54,000, a minor but sloppy inconsistency for an investor who calculates "to the last rupee." (4) setup_phase, review_checkpoints, rebalancing_guidelines, and projected_returns are entirely empty — the plan is structurally incomplete. (5) The rationale claims the S&P 500 fund is "partially boosted by rupee depreciation, which works IN your favour" — but doesn't address the symmetrical downside risk of INR appreciation on international returns. (6) The plan's stated philosophy emphasises Sharpe ratio optimisation, but the fund selection methodology relies purely on raw trailing returns — no risk adjustment is demonstrated anywhere.
